Facts.text: Pope Nicholas I answers the envoys of Boris (Ad consulta vestra)
English king Ethelred II (the 'Unready') launches 'St Brice's Day' massacre of Danish settlers
Pope Pius IV publishes decree Professi fidei
Battle of Sheriffmuir during Jacobite rebellion. Battle inconclusive but Government forces halt advance of Jacobite army lead by Scottish Earl of Mar
American Revolutionary forces capture Montreal
British troops occupy Negapatam, Ceylon (Sri Lanka)
1st US anti-slavery party, Liberty Party, convenes in NY
James Braid first sees a demonstration of animal magnetism, which leads to his study of the subject he eventually calls hypnosis
Peter Burnett elected first governor of California
1st meeting of anti-revolutionary "Netherlands & Orange"
Telegraph connection between London-Paris linked
The Denny Party lands at Alki Point, the first settlers of what would become Seattle, Washington
"New Era" sinks off New Jersey coast with loss of 300
A letter is written by Reverend Mark R. Watkinson petitioning the Treasury Department to "recognize Almighty God" on American coins; the department eventually decides on the motto "In God We Trust"
The new Constitution of Greece adopted
US issues 1st gold certificates
American Philological Association organized in NY
Harvard-Yale game is 1st college football contest with uniforms
National Bowling Association organized in NYC
Bloody Sunday clashes in central London
Baltimore Orioles (now NY Yankees) enter baseball's American League
Caister Lifeboat Disaster claims lives of nine lifeboat men off the coast of Norfolk, England
C. W. Gregory out for 383 as NSW make 763 v Queensland, in record cricket innings by an Australian
French cyclist Paul Cornu flies 1st helicopter (twin rotor)
The Conference of Central American States, convoked in response to the war between Honduras and Nicaragua, meets in Washington, D.C. to promote unification
259 miners die in a fire at St Paul Mine at Cherry, Illinois
Ben Simpson of Hamilton Tigers kicks 9 singles in 14-4 win over the Argos at Rosedale Field in Toronto
Collier's magazine accuses U.S. Secretary of the Interior Richard Ballinger of questionable dealings in Alaskan coal fields
British offensive at Ancre, Belgium
Prime Minister of Australia Billy Hughes is expelled from the Labor Party over his support for conscription
Prince Friedrich, last reigning Prince of Waldeck and Pyrmont, abdicates
Russia cancels Treaty of Brest-Litovsk
Stahlhelm forms (anti communist/Polish/French) in Magdenburg
Hudson River freezes at Albany
US, France, Japan and British Empire sign a Pacific Treaty
Indonesian Communist Party (PKI) uprising in Bantam, West Java
New York - New Jersey Holland Tunnel, 1st twin-tube underwater auto tunnel, opens
WA Drake's "Grand Hotel" premieres in NYC
Hattie Caraway (D-AK) appointed first US woman senator
First modern sit-down strike by Hormel meatpackers in Austin, Minnesota
Anti-British riots in Egypt
NBC forms first full-sized symphony orchestra exclusively for radio
America's first saint, Mother Frances Cabrini, beatified
U-81 torpedoes British aircraft carrier HMS Ark Royal near Gibraltar
Chaotic "sea battle of Friday the 13th" at Guadalcanal
Minimum draft age lowered from 21 to 18 in the US
Australian Services draw 1st Victory Test against India
First artificial snow produced from a natural cloud, Mt Greylock, Massachusetts
General Carlos Delgado Chalbaud, President of Venezuela, is assassinated in Caracas
US win 1st world championship bridge contest
Janet Collins is the first 1st African-American dancer to appear at the Metropolitan Opera, in a production of Aida
False fingernails 1st sold
KLBK TV channel 13 in Lubbock, TX (CBS) begins broadcasting
1st live telecast from non-contiguous foreign country-Havana Cuba
Argentine general Pedro Aramburu succeeds E Lonardi as president
US Supreme Court rules racial segregation on buses in Alabama unconstitutional
NYC Mayor Robert Wagner announces plans to begin a new baseball league called the Continental League, to fill the void created by the city's Giants and Dodgers moving to the west coast
The Academy of St Martin in the Fields' first professional concert takes place in London
Fire in movie theater kills 152 children in Amude, Spain
Vladimir Yefimovich Semichastny succeeds Aleksandr Nikolayevich Shelepin as head of the KGB
WCBB TV channel 10 in Augusta, ME (PBS) begins broadcasting
Bob Petit (St Louis Hawks) becomes 1st NBAer to score 20,000 points
"Yarmouth Castle" burns and sinks off Bahamas, killing 89
Director Kenneth Tynan says the word "F*ck" on BBC
Carl Stokes sworn-in as 1st major city black mayor (Cleveland, Ohio)
NL owners OK AL expansion to Seattle & Kansas City

William Craig, Home Affairs Minister, bans all marches, with the exception of 'customary' parades, in Derry, Northern Ireland; the exception of 'customary' parades meant that Loyalist institutions could parade but civil rights marches could not
US Vice PresidentSpiro Agnewcalls TV executives "impudent snobs"
Mariner 9, 1st to orbit another planet (Mars)
25th Islander shut-out Resch 6-0 Gilles scores on 5th penalty shot
Final Al Capp comic strip of "Li'l Abner" (1934-77)
NASA launches HEAO 2
British newspaper "The Times" resumes publishing after 1 year
US spacecraft Voyager 1 sends back first close-up pictures of Saturn during its fly-by
Vietnam Veterans Memorial opens in Washington, D.C., featuring the names of over 58,000 US soldiers killed or missing in the Vietnam War
David Levy finds his first comet
Nevado del Ruiz volcano erupts in Colombia, killing 25,000 in the town of Armero
NASA launches space vehicle S-199
USPresident Reaganconfesses weapon sales to Iran
1st condom commercial on British TV
In the New Zealand seaside town of Aramoana, David Gray shoots dead 13 people in the Aramoana massacre
Oakland's Bob Welch wins AL Cy Young Award
Saudis ask US for rights to bid on SPR (Strategic Petroleum Reserve) crude
Bomb destroys Dutch Labour party politician Aad Kosto's house in a failed assassination attempt, he survives and in a famous image finds and cuddles his cat
7.1 seaquake east of Kamchatka
Chinese MD82 makes crash landing at Urumqi, 12 killed
Pakistani minister of Foreign affairs Faruk Leghari elected president
Referendum is held for Sweden to join the European Union, resulting in 52.2% of voters in favor of joining
"Three Sisters" closes at Lunt-Fontanne Theater NYC
Joel Armengaud discovers 2^1398269 - 1 (35th known Mersenne prime)
Padres third baseman Ken Caminiti is 4th unanimous winner of NL MVP
"Lion King" opens at New Amersterdam Theater NYC
UN pulls out arms inspection teams from Iraq
The Beatles release their compilation album “1” (2001 Billboard Album of the Year), and launch their official website - thebeatles.com
Doha Round: The World Trade Organization ends a four-day ministerial conference in Doha, Qatar

War on Terrorism: In the first such act since World War II, US PresidentGeorge W. Bushsigns an executive order allowing military tribunals against foreigners suspected of connections to terrorist acts or planned acts on the United States.
Iraq disarmament crisis: Iraq agrees to the terms of the UN Security Council Resolution 1441
The oil tanker Prestige sinks off the Galician coast and causes a huge oil spill
An explosion hits the south wing of the House of Representatives of the Philippines in Quezon City, killing four people, including Congressman Wahab Akbar, and wounding six

Equity research by Deutsche Bank states that Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand and the Philippines should not experience a recession, despite potential harm to economic growth from falling commodity prices and possible weaker exports
Germany's economy, Europe's largest, contracts by 0.5% in the third quarter after GDP fell 0.4% in the second quarter, putting it in recession for the first time in five years

Germany's growth of 0.7% in the third quarter helps lead the Eurozone out of the recession after providing overall growth of 0.4% in the same period, with the whole European Union growing 0.2%, it is reported today

The Netherlands officially exits the recession after experiencing 0.4% growth in the third quarter, but recovery for the Netherlands still remains fragile as the country is highly dependent on exports to maintain the recovery
Australian rock band Powderfinger, perform their last concert at the Brisbane River Stage
3 Syrian tanks enter the demilitarized zone of Golan Heights
Clayton Kershaw and Mike Trout wins the MVP awards in MLB for 2014
Indian cricket batsman Rohit Sharma sets new world ODI record of 264 runs off 173 balls against Sri Lanka at Eden Gardens in Kolkata
Terror attacks in Paris at bars, restaurants, the national football stadium and Bataclan music venue leave 130 dead. Isis claim responsibility.
First Barbie doll to wear a hijab, modeled on Olympic fencer Ibtihaj Muhammad unveiled in New York
Italy fails to qualify for 2018 Football World Cup (first time since 1958), after play-off defeat against Sweden
World's earliest winemaking is uncovered in Khramis Didi Gora, Georgia, in clay pots from 6,000 BC
Amazon announces two new corporate outposts at Queens, New York and Arlington, Virginia
Four members of a family arrested for the Rhoden family massacre where eight people were shot, south of Columbus, Ohio in 2016
Kristine E. Guillaume is named the first black woman to head prestigious Harvard student newspaper "The Crimson"
Sri Lanka’s Supreme Court blocks President Maithripala Sirisena from dissolving parliament
Rediscovered painting "Lucretia" by female baroque artist Artemisia Gentileschi sells for $4.1 million to the Getty Museum at auction in Paris
Venice hit by worse floods for 50 years, due to a very high tide with waves in St Mark's square, killing one resident
Fly-half Nicolas Sanchez scores all the Pumas points as Argentina claim their 1st ever win over New Zealand's All Blacks, winning 25-15 in a Rugby Championship game in Sydney
Germany records its highest number of daily COVID-19 cases amid warnings its fourth wave could kill 100,000
Glasgow Climate Pact agreed at COP26: commits countries to a phasedown" of "unabated" coal, end deforestation by 2030 and cut methane emissions by 30% by 2030
Bombing on Istiklal Avenue, central Istanbul, Turkey, labelled a terrorist attack, killing six people and injuring 81
Four Idaho university students stabbed and killed in their home in Moscow, Idaho. Suspect Bryan Kohberger arrested seven weeks later.
ICC Men's Cricket T20 World Cup, Melbourne: England beats Pakistan by 5 wickets to win their WC second title; Player of the match & series: England fast bowler Sam Curran; crowd 80,462
The Deauville Hotel in Miami Beach, Florida, built in 1957 and host to JFK, The Beatles, and Frank Sinatra is imploded; closed in 2017 after an electrical fire
Kenya holds its first national tree planting holiday, aiming to plant 100 million trees. Part of a climate change initiative to plant 15 billion trees by 2032
Breakaway region Somaliland elects as its 6th president Abdirahman Mohamed Abdullahi, succeeding current president Muse Bihi Abdi
World's largest coral, larger than a blue whale and 300 years old, found off the Solomon Islands in the southwest Pacific Ocean
Louis the Pious, King of the Franks, performs public act of penance at the Church of Saint Medard in Soissons
English LadyJane Greyand Archbishop Thomas Cranmer accused of high treason
KingPhilip II's "Letters From the Segovia Woods", rejecting requests to abolish ordinances outlawing heresy, reach Brussels
Battle at Turnham Green outside London: KingCharles Ivs English parliament
Christopher Wrenis appointed architect for the rebuilding of St Paul's Cathedral in London by KingCharles II
In a letter to Jean-Baptiste Le Roy,Benjamin Franklinwrites "in this world nothing can be said to be certain, except death and taxes"
"Heart of Darkness" byJoseph Conradis first published in one volume (previously serialized) by William Blackwood in Edinburgh
Walt Disney's animated film "Fantasia," starring Leopold Stokowski, The Philadelphia Orchestra, Mickey Mouse, and ballet-dancing hippopotamuses, premieres at the Broadway Theatre, NYC
German military-intelligence service Abwehr meets with Yugoslav Chetnik guerilla leader Draza Mihailovic
Lefty O'Doul's all-stars, includingJoe DiMaggioandBilly Martin, lose 3-1 to Pacific League all-star team (Japan)
Dmitri Shostakovich's 4th String Quartet premieres
PopePaul VIgives up the jewel-encrusted gold papal crown, donating it to the poor at a service in Rome
American comedian Flip Wilson makes his debut appearance on "TheEd SullivanShow"; he returns 10 times
As a rookie, future Basketball Hall of Fame forwardElvin Hayesscores 54 points in San Diego Rockets' 122-120 win over Detroit Pistons, a career-high
MLB St. Louis Cardinals pitcher Bob Gibson edges Cincinnati RedsPete Roseto win NL MVP
Vice PresidentSpiro Agnewaccuses network TV news departments of bias and distortion
Steven Spielbergmakes his feature-length film debut with the made-for-television movie "Duel"
Oakland A'sReggie Jacksonwins AL MVP unanimously
DodgersSteve Garveywins NL MVP
Pittsburgh Pirates outfielderWillie Stargelland St Louis Cardinals first basemanKeith Hernandezshare National League MVP Award
Ronald Reaganannounces his candidacy for US President in NYC
Ringo Starrreleases single "Wrack My Brain", written and produced byGeorge Harrison, in the UK

WBA lightweight championRay Mancinidefeats South Korean challenger Duk Koo Kim by TKO in the 14th round in Las Vegas; Kim collapses, falls into a coma, and dies four days later; as a result, WBC shortens title bouts to 12 rounds; WBA and WBO follow in 1988, and IBF in 1989
Chicago Cubs infielderRyne Sandbergwins the NL MVP Award
Dwight Gooden, youngest 20 game winner, wins Cy Young award
Sonny &Cherperform together for the final time, singing "I Got You Babe" on 'Late Night withDavid Letterman' (NBC)
Brazilian McLaren driverAyrton Sennafinishes 2nd in the season ending Australian Grand Prix in Adelaide to win his first Formula 1 World Drivers Championship; wins title by 3 points fromAlain Prost
Paul McCartneyreleases single "Figure of 8" & "Ou Est Le Soleil"
Boston Red SoxRoger Clemenswins AL Cy Young Award
Riddick Bowewins the undisputed world heavyweight boxing crown with a unanimous points decision overEvander Holyfieldin Las Vegas; first of their 3 meetings

Title contendersMichael Schumacherand Damon Hill famously collide on lap 35 of the season ending Australian Grand Prix in Adelaide; Schumacher wins his first F1 World Drivers Championship by 1 point from Hill
"GoldenEye", 17th James Bond film released, starringPierce Brosnanfor the first time,Robbie ColtraneandJudi Denchas M
Ken Griffey Jr.unanimously wins AL MVP
"Toy Story2", directed by John Lasseter, starringTom HanksandTim Allen, premieres in the US
Lennox LewisdefeatsEvander Holyfieldin 12 rounds by unanimous decision to become the undisputed heavyweight champion of the world
Philippine House Speaker Manuel B. Villar, Jr. passes the articles of impeachment against Philippine PresidentJoseph Estrada
Colombian singer-songwriterShakira's fifth studio album and first English language album, "Laundry Service", is released
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushorders that the Strategic Petroleum Reserve be filled to capacity over the next few years
Eminemreleases the single 'Lose Yourself' from the soundtrack of "8 Mile", the first rap song to win the Academy Award for Best Original Song
French tennis starAmélie Mauresmobeats countrywomanMary Pierce5–7, 7–6, 6–4 for the season ending WTA Tour Championship at the Staples Center, Los Angeles
Chandra Bahadur Dangimeets the world's tallest living man,Sultan Kösen, at an event in London for Guinness World Records Day
Bolivian opposition senator Jeanine Áñez assumes interim presidency of Bolivia afterEvo Morales' resignation
First day of public testimony inDonald Trump's impeachment inquiry held in Washington, D.C.
South Korean girl groupBlackpinkare the first K-pop group to have a single reach 1 billion views on YouTube for "Ddu-Du Ddu-Du"
British pop singerHarry Stylesbecomes the first-ever solo male featured on the cover US Vogue magazine
British strategist and chief advisor to the PM Dominic Cummings is let go byBoris Johnson
Kylie Minoguebecomes the first woman to top the UK album chart over five consecutive decades with "Disco"
Joel Embiidscores a career-high 59 points in a victory over the Utah Jazz
Former British PMDavid Cameronmakes shock return to government as Foreign Secretary, after PMRishi Sunaksacks Suella Braverman and appoints James Cleverly Home Secretary
Historical epic film "Gladiator II," directed and produced byRidley Scottand starring Paul Mescal,Pedro Pascal, andDenzel Washington, receives a royal premiere with KingCharles IIIin London
Last bull run in Britain as the Stamford bull run ends after 700 years
The Great Bell of Big Ben first chimes at the foot of the still-unfinished clock tower outside the Palace of Westminster
Trial of Mexican drug lordJoaquín "El Chapo" Guzmánbegins in New York, he is charged with drug trafficking, money laundering, and murder
Augustine of Hippo, North African Christian theologian and philosopher, born in Thagaste, Numidia (d. 430)
Johann Eck, German Scholastic theologian and early counter-reformer against Lutheran challenges, born in Egg, Swabia (d. 1543)
Philip I (the Generous), Landgrave of Hesse and important early protestant ruler, born in Marburg, Landgraviate of Hesse, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1567)
Albert VII, Archduke of Austria, born in Wiener Neustadt, Austria (d. 1621)
Cyril Lucaris, Greek theologian and Patriarch of Constantinople, born in Heraklion, Greece (d. 1638)
Árni Magnússon, Icelandic scholar and collector of manuscripts (Arnamagnæan Manuscript Collection), born in Kvennabrekka, Iceland (d. 1730)
Thomas Philip Wallrad de Hénin-Liétard d'Alsace, Cardinal- Archbishop of Mechelen, born in Brussels, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1759)
Jan Zach, Czech violinist, organist, and composer, baptized in Čelákovice, Bohemia (d. 1773)
Charles Simon Favart, French dramatist, born in Paris (d. 1792)
William Shenstone, English poet, born in Leasowes, England (d. 1763)
Dorothea Erxleben, German first female medical doctor, born in Quedlinburg, Germany (d. 1762)
Anton Laube, Bohemian composer, born in Brüx (d. 1784)
John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich, British statesman who claimed to have invented the sandwich, born in Chiswick, England (d. 1792)
Valentin Haüy, French professor "interpreter to the King", founded the first school for the blind, born in Saint-Just-en-Chaussée, France (d. 1822)[1]
Timothy Olmstead, American fifer and composer, born in East Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British America (d. 1848)
Jiaqing [Yongyan], 5th Emperor of Qing Dynasty (1796-1820), born at the Old Summer Palace, near Beijing, China (d. 1820)
Esaias Tegnér, Swedish writer considered the father of modern Swedish poetry (Frithjof's Saga), born in Kyrkerud, Sweden (d. 1846)
Caroline Lamb, English aristocrat, author (Glenarvon), wife of William Lamb and lover of Lord Byron, born in London (d. 1828)
Jean Martin de Ron, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1817)
Edward John Trelawny, English traveler and author (Adv of Younger Son), born in London (d. 1881)
Amalie Auguste of Bavaria, Queen consort of Saxony (1854-73), born in Munich, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1877)
Elisabeth Ludovika of Bavaria, Queen consort of Prussia (1840-61), born in Munich, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1873)
Theophilus Hunter Holmes, Lieutenant General (Confederate Army), born in Sampson County, North Carolina (d. 1880)
John A. Dahlgren, US Navy officer and inventor (Civil war Dahlgren-cannon), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1870)
Yuri Karlovich Arnold, Russian composer, born in St. Petersburg, Petrograd (d. 1898)
John Wolcott Phelps, American abolitionist, author and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Guilford County, Vermont (d. 1885)
Peter II, prince-bishop of Montenegro (1830-51) and poet (The Ray of The Microcosm), born in Njeguši, Montenegro (d. 1851)
Joseph Hooker, American Major General (Union Army), born in Hadley, Massachusetts (d. 1879)
Ignacio Manuel Altamirano, Mexican author (El Zarco), born in Tixtla, Guerrero, Mexico (d. 1893)
James T. Rapier, African-American politician, born in Florence, Alabama (d. 1883)
Joseph F. Smith, 6th President of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, born in Far West, Missouri (d. 1918)
Edward Burd Grubb, American Civil War Union Brevet Brigadier General, born in Burlington, New Jersey (d. 1913)
Albert I, Prince of Monaco (1889-1922), born in Paris (d. 1922)
John Drew Jr., American Shakespearean actor (The Big Bonanza), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1927)
George Whitfield Chadwick, American composer (Judi Van Winke), born in Lowell, Massachusetts (d. 1931)
Daniel François Scheurleer, Dutch musicologist and banker, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1927)
Louis Brandeis, American lawyer and associate justice on the Supreme Court of the United States (1916-39), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1941)
Sigwart Aspestrand, Norwegian composer, born in Fredrikshald, Norway (d. 1941)
Georg Knorr, German engineer (brake system trains), born in Ruda bei Neumark, West Prussia (d. 1911)
Percy McDonnell, Australian cricket batsman (19 Tests, 3 x 100s; Victoria CA, NSW CA, Queensland CA), born in London, England (d. 1896)
Abraham Flexner, American educator, born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1959)
Pierre Maurice, Swiss composer, born in Allaman, Switzerland (d. 1936)
Ariadna Tyrkova-Williams, Russian politician, writer, and feminist, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1962)
Helene Stöcker, German feminist, pacifist, and writer, born in Elberfeld, Germany (d. 1943)
Henry Kolker, German-American actor and director (Holiday, Baby Face, Mad Love), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1947)
Marguerite Long, French concert pianist (Ravel) and teacher (Paris Conservatoire, 1906-40), born in Nîmes, France (d. 1966)
Rogelio del Villar, Spanish composer, born in Leon, Spain (d. 1937)
Johannes Kielstra, Dutch economist, governor of Suriname (1933-44), and diplomat, born in Zwartsluis, Netherlands (d. 1951)
Max Dehn, German mathematician (Dehn’s lemma), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1952)
Maurice Delage, French pianist, and composer (Ragamalika; Seven Haikais), born in Paris, France (d. 1961)
John Lowry, American NYC builder (Radio City Music Hall), born in Mount Vernon, New York (d. 1962)
Montague Fawcett Phillips, British composer, born in Tottenham, London (d. 1969)
Florence Moore, American vaudeville, stage and silent screen actress, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1935)
Mary Wigman, German dancer, choreographer, and teacher, born in Hanover, Germany (d. 1973)
Bennie Moten, American jazz pianist and bandleader (Moten's Kansas City Orchestra - "South", "Moten Swing"), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1935)[1]
Edward A. Doisy Sr, American bio-chemist (Vitamin K1, Nobel 1943), born in Hume, Illinois (d. 1986)
Arthur Nebe, German SS officer and Holocaust perpetrator who took part in assassination plot against of Adolf Hitler, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1945)
Nita Naldi [Anita Donna Dooley], American stage and silent film actress (Blood & Sand; Dr. Jekyll & Mr. Hyde), born in New York City (d. 1961)
Nobusuke Kishi, Prime Minister of Japan (1957-60), born in Yamaguchi, Japan (d. 1987)
Gertrude Olmstead, American silent film actress, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1975)
Howard Hill, American expert bowman (billed as "The World's Greatest Archer"; movie technical adviser), born in Wilsonville, Alabama (d. 1975)
Iskandar Ali Mirza, Bengali-Pakistani general and 1st President of Pakistan (1956-58), born in Murshidabad, Bengal Presidency, British India (d. 1969)
Martin Bodmer, Swiss Bibliophile and scholar (Bodmer Papyri), born in Zurich, Switzerland (d. 1971)
Edward Buzzell, American film director (Child of Manhattan), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1985)
Gustav von Koenigswald, German-Dutch paleontologist (found Pithecanthropus erectus), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1982)
Sid Stone, American comedian (Milton Berle Show), born in New York City (d. 1986)
Conrad Thibault, American singer (Jacques Fray Music Room), born in Northbridge, Massachusetts (d. 1987)
Eva Zeisel, American industrial designer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2011)
Hermione Baddeley, British actress (Camp Runamuck, Maude, Good Life), born in Broseley, England (d. 1986)
Giovanna of Savoy, Italian princess, and Tsaritsa of Bulgaria (1930-43), born in Rome, Kingdom of Italy (d. 2000)
Josef Kjellgren, Swedish writer (Guldkedjan), born in Mörkö, Södermanland, Sweden (d. 1948)
Lewis Boddington, Welsh aerospace engineer (RAE), born in Merthyr Tydfil, Wales (d. 1994)
C. Vann Woodward, American historian and educator, born in Vanndale, Arkansas (d. 1999)
Gunnar Bjornstrand, Swedish actor (Shame, Dreams), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1986)
Paul de Vree, Flemish author (Between Doubt & Tears), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1982)
William Bradford Huie, American writer and publisher, born in Hartselle, Alabama (d. 1986)
Alexander Scourby, American actor (Jesus, Shaggy Dog, Giant), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1985)
Helen Mack, American actress (Son of Kong, Milky Way), born in Rock Island, Illinois (d. 1986)
Lon Nol, Cambodian politician, 7th President of the Khmer Republic (1972-75), born in Kampong Leav, Prey Veng, Cambodia (d. 1985)
Malcom McLean, American trucking fleet owner (SeaLand - invented containerized shipping), born near Maxton, North Carolina (d. 2001)[1]
Alberto Lattuada, Italian film director, born in Vaprio d'Adda, Lombardy, Italy (d. 2005)
Howard Cooke, Governor-General of Jamaica (1991-2006), born in Goodwill, St. James, Jamaica (d. 2014)
Robert Sterling, American actor (Manhattan Heartbeat; Topper (TV series)), born in New Castle, Pennsylvania (d. 2006)
Janine Andrade, French classical violinist, born in Besançon, France (d. 1997)
Jack Elam, American actor (The Dakotas, East Street, Rio Lobo), born in Miami, Arizona (d. 2003)
Eddie Calhoun, American jazz bassist, born in Clarksdale, Mississippi (d. 1994)
Joonas Kokkonen, Finnish composer (The Last Temptations), born in Iisalmi, Finland (d. 1996)
Yoshiro Irino, Japanese composer, born in Vladivostok, Russia (d. 1980)
Jack Narz, American TV game show host (Dotto, Video Village), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 2008)
Madeleine Sherwood, Canadian stage and screen actress (Reverend Mother Placido in  "The Flying Nun"), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2016)
Oskar Werner [Bschließmayer], Austrian stage and screen actor an(The Spy Who Came In from the Cold; Ship of Fools; Fahrenheit 451), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1984)
Arthur Hiller, Canadian director (Love Story), born in Edmonton, Alberta (d. 2016)
Linda Christian, Mexican actress (Athena, VIPs, Battle Zone), born in Tampico, Mexico (d. 2011)
Motoo Kimura, Japanese geneticist, born in Okazaki, Japan (d. 1994)
Inez van Dullemen, Dutch author (Vroeger is dood), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2021)
Don Gordon [Guadagno], American actor (Bullitt; Papillon), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2017)

Max Vernon Mathews, American composer, born in Columbus, Nebraska (d. 2011)
Billy Klüver, American scientist and engineer, born in Monaco (d. 2004)
John Pont, American college football coach (Miami, Yale, Northwestern, Indiana), born in Canton, Ohio (d. 2008)
Hampton Hawes, American jazz pianist, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1977)
Fred Phelps, American Baptist minister and civil rights attorney, born in Meridian, Mississippi (d. 2014)
Fred Harris, American politician (US Senator from Oklahoma, 1964-73), born in Walters, Oklahoma (d. 2024)
Nico Scheepmaker, Dutch columnist and poet, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1990)
Andrée Lachapelle, French-Canadian stage and screen actress (The Last Escape), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2019)
Anthony Jacobs, English businessman (Nig Securities Group; British School of Motoring) and politician, born in UK (d. 2014)
Joan Lestor, British Labour politician, born in Vancouver, British Columbia (d. 1998)
John Manke, American USMC fighter pilot and NASA test pilot (HL-10), born in Selby, South Dakota (d. 2019)[1]
Olga Connolly-Fikotova, Czech-American discus thrower (Olympic gold, 1956), born in Libiš, Czech Republic (d. 2024)
Richard Mulligan, American Emmy Award-winning actor (Soap - "Burt"; Empty Nest; The Big Bus), born in The Bronx, New York City (d. 2000)
Adrienne Corri, actress (River, Doctor Zhivago), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2016)
Peter Arnett, New Zealand-born American journalist, born in Riverton, New Zealand
George Carey, Archbishop of Canterbury, born in The East End, Co. London
Michael Getler, American journalist and 1st ombudsman appointed by a US broadcasting network (PBS), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 2018)
Thomas Augustine Martin, Irish academic, born in Ballinamore, County Leitrim (d. 1995)
Tom Atkins, American actor (Escape from New York), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
Kamahl [Kandiah Kamalesvaran], Australian singer (Elephant Song), born in Kuala Lumpur
Tabu Ley Rochereau [Pascal-Emmanuel Sinamoyi Tabu], African musician (Orchestre Afrisa International), born in Bagata, Bandundu, Belgian Congo (d. 2013)
Adrian Barber, British musician, recording engineer, and producer (Allman Brothers; Velvet Underground; Aerosmith), born in Ilkley, Yorkshire, England (d. 2020)
Gérald Godin, French Canadian poet and politician, born in Trois-Rivières, Quebec (d. 1994)
Jean Seberg, American actress (Breathless, Paint Your Wagon), born in Marshaltown, Iowa (d. 1979)
Julio Canani, Peruvian thoroughbred horse racing trainer (Breeders' Cup Mile 1999, 2001; Juvenile Fillies 2004), born in Oxampampa, Peru (d. 2021)
